metadata.title: Black-Scholes Option Pricer — Call, Put, and Greeks with Claude
metadata.note: CLI video spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes
beats.narration_text: Every option contract has a fair price — and for decades, nobody could calculate it rigorously. Then Black, Scholes, and Merton published a formula in 1973. Five inputs: spot price, strike, time to expiry, risk-free rate, volatility. One formula. It transformed finance. But the formula also comes with risk sensitivities — the Greeks — that tell you exactly how exposed you are. Today we build the pricer from scratch with Claude, verify it at par, and read the Greeks off the table.
beats.visual_intent: title card: Black-Scholes formula with the five inputs labeled; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt into the terminal. We ask Claude to write black_scholes.py, computing call and put prices plus the five main Greeks — Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega, Rho — for strikes from 80 to 120, T equals 0.5 years, r equals 5 percent, sigma equals 20 percent.

props.command:
claude "Write black_scholes.py computing call/put prices and Greeks
(Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega) using scipy.stats.norm for
S=100, K=80..120 step 5, T=0.5, r=0.05, sigma=0.2.
Print table: Strike | Call | Put | Delta | Gamma | Theta | Vega"

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The call price is S times N of d1, minus K times the discount factor times N of d2. d1 and d2 come from log of S over K, plus or minus a half sigma squared times T, all divided by sigma root T. Delta is just N of d1 — the probability the option expires in the money under the risk-neutral measure. That single line encodes the whole formula.

props.code:
# black_scholes.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm

def black_scholes(S, K, T, r, sigma):
    d1 = (np.log(S/K) + (r + 0.5*sigma**2)*T) / (sigma*np.sqrt(T))
    d2 = d1 - sigma*np.sqrt(T)
    call = S*norm.cdf(d1) - K*np.exp(-r*T)*norm.cdf(d2)
    put  = K*np.exp(-r*T)*norm.cdf(-d2) - S*norm.cdf(-d1)
    delta = norm.cdf(d1)          # call delta
    gamma = norm.pdf(d1)/(S*sigma*np.sqrt(T))
    vega  = S*norm.pdf(d1)*np.sqrt(T)
    return call, put, delta, gamma, vega

beats.narration_text: Run it. The crimson curve is the Black-Scholes call price — it bends above the grey hockey stick, which is the intrinsic value. At the money, strike 100, the option is worth ten dollars and forty-five cents. Below the strike the curve hugs the floor; above it climbs linearly as the option goes deep in the money. The curve is smooth and convex — that convexity is Gamma.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: Call price curve (crimson) over spot prices 60-140, with intrinsic value hockey stick (grey dashed), ATM marker, and strike line at K=100

beats.narration_text: Check: does the at-the-money call actually equal the put? Put-call parity says they should differ only by the present value of the forward. Let's ask Claude to add a parity verification column and flag any violations.

props.command:
claude "update black_scholes.py:
  -> add put-call parity check column
  -> verify C - P = S - K*exp(-r*T) for each strike
  -> flag any parity violation > 0.001"

beats.narration_text: Put-call parity holds to the penny — every row passes. The model is internally consistent. The Greeks tell you the risk: Delta near 0.5 at the money, Gamma highest at the money, Vega proportional to time value. Five numbers, one decision.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: parity check table; Greeks labeled and interpreted

beats.narration_text: Your move: swap in implied volatility — invert the formula to back out sigma from a market price. That is the number traders actually trade. And when you do, plot the vol smile across strikes — you will see that Black-Scholes is not quite right, and that gap is where the real pricing models live.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: implied vol inversion, vol smile plot; pointer to Monte Carlo reel

beats.narration_text: Try it on real options data from yfinance. Pull the chain, compute implied vols, and plot the smile. The skew you see is the market pricing in crash risk that Black-Scholes ignores.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: yfinance options chain pull + implied vol solver

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
